Zone: Remote Integration (`packages-rex:remote-integration`)

</imports>

<findings>

[suggestion] [info] Zone "Remote Integration" (packages-rex:remote-integration) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.75, cohesion: 0.25, coupling: 0.75) — justified: After pinning tree/DAG primitives to prd-operations-core, acknowledge.ts to prd-operations-core, recommend types/creators to prd-operations-core, and CLI commands to mutation-commands, the zone contracts to its true domain: remote adapter registry, Notion client, sync engine, and conflict detection. Remaining coupling is expected for integration infrastructure.

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.25) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.75) — 32 imports target "prd-operations-core"
- 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- The adapter-registry, notion-client, and integration-schema form a coherent remote-sync subsystem, but they are co-mingled with core graph utilities (dag.ts, tree.ts, canonical.ts) that belong closer to the domain foundation.
- Extracting the remote adapter infrastructure (adapter-registry, notion-client, integration-schema) into its own zone would improve cohesion and make the sync boundary explicit.
- The bidirectional import relationship with the core zone (core → unit: 33, unit → core: 32) suggests shared primitives are scattered across both zones rather than consolidated in one place.
- Cohesion of 0.25 and coupling of 0.75 indicate this zone is a grab-bag of loosely related modules that the community-detection algorithm could not place elsewhere — a structural refactor signal.
- Bidirectional imports with the core zone (33 outbound, 32 inbound) create a near-circular dependency; graph primitives like dag.ts and tree.ts should have a clear directional home.
- The Notion client and adapter registry are the only external-service integration points in the codebase — isolating them behind a dedicated gateway would make the integration surface auditable.
- Zone "remote-integration" has files across 11 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- remote-integration appears in bidirectional coupling with prd-analysis-pipeline, mutation-commands, data-persistence-validation, rex-status-mcp-cli, and notion-integration simultaneously — it functions as a second hub rather than a leaf integration layer, which contradicts its name and intended role.
- The zone's 11-directory span combined with its hub-like coupling pattern suggests it accumulated responsibilities organically; the algorithmic zone boundary drawn here is not a design boundary but an artifact of graph partitioning on residual files.
- remote-integration acts as a second gravity well alongside core — it has bidirectional coupling with every major production zone, making it impossible to evolve the remote sync subsystem without touching the analysis pipeline, mutation commands, and MCP layer simultaneously.
- Extracting dag.ts, tree.ts, and canonical.ts into the core zone and isolating notion-client and adapter-registry into a dedicated integrations/ zone would dissolve this artificial hub and restore remote-integration to its intended role as a leaf.
- acknowledge.ts lives in src/analyze/ (analysis pipeline directory) but the zone algorithm placed it in remote-integration because recommend.ts imports it — the file's actual responsibility is persisting acknowledged findings to a separate JSON store, making it a store-layer module misclassified twice: once by directory, once by zone assignment.
- acknowledge.ts manages its own persistence store (acknowledged-findings.json) using raw fs/promises calls rather than routing through file-adapter.ts — a second independent persistence mechanism exists outside the store zone with no shared abstraction.
- src/analyze/acknowledge.ts is a store-layer module (owns acknowledged-findings.json, exports AcknowledgedStore/AcknowledgedFinding, performs atomic reads/writes) co-located in the analyze/ directory and zone-assigned to remote-integration solely because recommend.ts imports it. It belongs in src/store/ alongside file-adapter.ts.
- acknowledge.ts implements its own file I/O (readFile/writeFile) in parallel with atomic-write.ts and file-adapter.ts — two independent persistence implementations exist with no shared abstraction, meaning concurrency and error-handling improvements must be applied in multiple places.
- The src/core/ directory is physically split across two zones: canonical.ts, dag.ts, move.ts, and tree.ts reside in src/core/ but are zone-assigned to remote-integration, while the remaining core/ files belong to prd-operations-core. A developer navigating src/core/ by directory cannot determine zone membership without consulting the zone map — the directory name provides false assurance of uniform architectural role.
- The src/store/ directory conflates two unrelated concern groups: persistence infrastructure (file-adapter.ts, atomic-write.ts, project-config.ts, contracts.ts) and integration adapters (notion-client.ts, notion-adapter.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schema.ts, integration-schemas/). The 'store' name correctly describes the first group and actively misrepresents the second; adapter files would be better located in src/integrations/.
- src/core/ is split between remote-integration (canonical.ts, dag.ts, move.ts, tree.ts) and prd-operations-core — the directory name implies zone unity that does not exist. Consolidate all cross-cutting graph primitives (dag.ts, canonical.ts, tree.ts) into prd-operations-core/src/core/ or extract them into a dedicated src/graph/ directory to restore directory-as-zone alignment.
- src/store/ contains 7 integration adapter files (notion-client.ts, notion-adapter.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schema.ts, integration-schemas/) alongside 5 persistence infrastructure files. These two groups should be split into src/store/ (persistence only) and src/integrations/ (adapters only) to eliminate the directory-level naming mismatch and give integration code a coherent home.
- [call graph] 676 internal calls, 78 outgoing, 218 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

</insights>
